summary: McKenzie reflects on his growing dependency on Google for his Bingo Card Creator business, noting that Google properties account for 65% of his web traffic and over half of his sales. He acknowledges that while this dependency is manageable because he has a day job as a safety net, it's concerning for his long-term goal of making his business his primary income. He evaluates several mitigation strategies including diversifying ad providers, maintaining backup payment systems, and finding non-search customer acquisition channels. The post concludes with the uncomfortable realization that even word-of-mouth referrals ultimately funnel back through Google search.
key_points: Google properties account for 65% of web traffic and over half of sales, creating dangerous single-vendor dependency
Having a day job provides a safety net that makes the risk tolerable but doesn't solve the underlying problem for someone planning to go full-time
Backup plans for payment processing are feasible (45-second recovery to PayPal), but backup plans for organic search traffic are essentially impossible
Even diversified customer acquisition channels like word-of-mouth ultimately route back through Google search
Microsoft's algorithm changes already demonstrated how a single search engine tweak can damage valuable rankings
money_quotes: I'm dependent on Google. Google properties send me 65% of my web traffic and account for over half of my sales.
If Google ever tweaks their algorithms in a similar fashion, I'm 31 flavors of screwed.
The idea of having my business at the mercy of one player who is not me is rather disconcerting.
Bingo Card Creator could close up shop tomorrow night and while I'd regret it I'd still be able to pay the rent, buy food, and save for retirement.
In the event of catastrophic failure at Google Checkout I can be recovered in under 45 seconds.
Unfortunately, when my users tell their friends to go download Bingo Card Creator, their friends Google for it. D'oh!
key_insight: Platform dependency on a single traffic source is an existential business risk that even diversified acquisition strategies struggle to mitigate when that platform dominates the entire discovery layer.
